qa_pairs.question: What are Meltdown and Spectre?
qa_pairs.answer: Meltdown and Spectre are bugs related to speculative execution in various CPU architectures, developed over the past ten to fifteen years, primarily affecting Intel and AMD processors, including those on Compute Canada clusters.

qa_pairs.question: Which CPU processors are particularly affected by Meltdown and Spectre?
qa_pairs.answer: Processors from Intel and AMD are particularly affected by Meltdown and Spectre.

qa_pairs.question: Are processors on Compute Canada clusters affected by Meltdown and Spectre?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, processors in use on Compute Canada clusters are affected by Meltdown and Spectre.

qa_pairs.question: Where can I find a detailed discussion about Meltdown and Spectre?
qa_pairs.answer: A detailed discussion of Meltdown and Spectre can be found on this page: https://arstechnica.com/gadgets/2018/01/meltdown-and-spectre-every-modern-processor-has-unfixable-security-flaws/.

qa_pairs.question: Has Compute Canada patched systems vulnerable to Meltdown and Spectre?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, Compute Canada personnel have patched systems deemed sensitive to these vulnerabilities.

qa_pairs.question: What was required to patch the Meltdown and Spectre vulnerabilities?
qa_pairs.answer: Patching the Meltdown and Spectre vulnerabilities required updating the operating system and rebooting the nodes.

qa_pairs.question: How were compute nodes updated for Meltdown and Spectre vulnerabilities?
qa_pairs.answer: For compute nodes, updates were typically done in a rolling fashion, were largely transparent to users, and are now complete.

qa_pairs.question: When were updates applied at Graham for these vulnerabilities?
qa_pairs.answer: Updates were applied at Graham between 2018 January 5 and January 31, with most nodes updated by January 13.

qa_pairs.question: Have benchmarks been run to evaluate the performance effects of the Meltdown and Spectre patches?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, many groups around the world have run benchmarks to evaluate the effects of the operating system patches on performance.

qa_pairs.question: What is the potential performance impact cited for the security patches?
qa_pairs.answer: Performance impacts range from very minimal to alarming figures, with some citations indicating up to a 30% or even 50% performance hit.

qa_pairs.question: Which types of tasks are most heavily affected by the security patches' performance impact?
qa_pairs.answer: Tasks which involve a lot of input/output (reading and writing files) seem to be most heavily affected. Examples include databases, or file transfers (e.g. rsync).

qa_pairs.question: How are most high performance computing (HPC) jobs affected by the security patches?
qa_pairs.answer: Most high performance computing jobs should be minimally affected since the vast majority of the run time is spent computing rather than doing input and output.

qa_pairs.question: Do different processor generations experience varying degrees of performance degradation from the patches?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, different processor generations are affected to different degrees, with the most notable performance degradation reported for older processors.

qa_pairs.question: Where can I find performance comparisons related to these patches?
qa_pairs.answer: You can find links to some recent performance comparisons in the References section below.

qa_pairs.question: Are the referenced performance comparisons necessarily reflective of Compute Canada clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: No, the referenced performance comparisons were not necessarily run on hardware and operating systems similar to what Compute Canada clusters are running.

qa_pairs.question: What is Compute Canada doing about the Meltdown and Spectre vulnerabilities?
qa_pairs.answer: All vulnerable equipment operated by Compute Canada has been patched, and if and when vendors release new patches, these will also be applied.

qa_pairs.question: What assurance does Compute Canada provide regarding system security?
qa_pairs.answer: Compute Canada team members are taking every action possible to ensure that the systems they run are secure.

qa_pairs.question: Who is responsible for updating virtual machines running on the Compute Canada cloud?
qa_pairs.answer: If you are operating your own virtual machine in our cloud, you are responsible for updating its operating system to include the latest security patches.

qa_pairs.question: Who should I contact if I believe my application's performance is severely impacted by the security patches?
qa_pairs.answer: If you believe that your application may be severely impacted by the security patches, you should contact our Technical support team.

qa_pairs.question: What kind of information should I provide to the Technical support team regarding performance impacts?
qa_pairs.answer: You are encouraged to bring forward comparative performance numbers of your application, such as job run times before and after the announcement.

qa_pairs.question: Is it always possible to mitigate the performance impact of the security patches?
qa_pairs.answer: No, mitigating the performance impact of the security patches is likely to require some modification to the code you are running, and may not always be possible.

qa_pairs.question: How should I maintain my virtual machine on the Compute Canada Cloud regarding security patches?
qa_pairs.answer: You should update your virtual machine's operating system to the latest version frequently to ensure it has the latest security patches to address these bugs.

qa_pairs.question: Where can I find specific instructions for updating Linux VMs?
qa_pairs.answer: Specific instructions on how to update Linux VMs can be found by referring to 'updating your VM'.

qa_pairs.question: What kind of resources are provided in the References section?
qa_pairs.answer: The References section provides links to general information about Spectre and Meltdown, initial and further benchmarks of performance impacts, whitepapers, advisories, tools, and academic papers related to these bugs.
